17: A type: 'Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.' Jesus himself identifies this as the OT's appointed sign for his resurrection: 'For just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matthew 12:40). The typological elements: Jonah goes into the deep as a substitute (the sailors are saved from the storm by throwing Jonah overboard; Jonah 1:12-15) → Jonah is entombed in the fish → Jonah emerges to complete his mission to the Gentiles. The antitype: Christ goes to the cross as the world's substitute → Christ is entombed → Christ rises and commissions the mission to all Gentiles (Matthew 28:19). Jonah's Gentile mission to Nineveh is the shadow; the apostolic mission to all nations is the substance.
1: A theme: the prophetic commissioning that opens Jonah participates in the pattern of YHWH sending his word through a human messenger to a people who need to hear it. The entire prophetic tradition — every “the word of the LORD came” — points toward the Word who comes in person: “In many and various ways God spoke to our fathers through the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us through his Son” (Hebrews 1:1-2). Jonah is the last in a long line of those to whom the word came; Christ is the Word.
2: A revelation of God: YHWH's concern is not confined to Israel — it extends to Nineveh, the capital of the most powerful and most feared empire of the day, because “their wickedness has come up before me.” This reveals the God of universal moral sovereignty who cares about the conduct of all nations. The NT presents this character as expressed definitively in Christ: “For God so loved the world” (John 3:16) — the same God who was troubled by Nineveh's wickedness sent his Son not to condemn the world but to save it (John 3:17). The Great Commission (Matthew 28:19) is the Jonah-commission fully obeyed.
3: A type by contrast: Jonah's flight from YHWH's presence is the anti-shape that Christ fills positively. Where Jonah runs to Tarshish (the farthest known point west), Christ “set his face to go to Jerusalem” (Luke 9:51) — the city of his death — and went willingly. Where Jonah “went down” (the verb yarad appears three times in v3) in a trajectory of descent from the call, Christ's descent was voluntary and purposeful: from heaven to manger to Galilee to Jerusalem to Gethsemane to the cross. The contrast clarifies what faithful obedience looks like — and underlines the costliness of what Christ did where Jonah did not.
4: A shadow: YHWH “hurled a great wind upon the sea” to pursue the fleeing prophet — the storm as the instrument of divine discipline. In the NT, Christ's disciples face a storm on the Sea of Galilee with Jesus asleep in the boat (Mark 4:37-38). The contrast is sharp: in Jonah, the storm is divine judgment directed at the guilty prophet; in Mark, the storm threatens the disciples traveling with the innocent one. Christ's authority over the wind and sea (“Peace! Be still!”) demonstrates that the God who commanded the storm in Jonah is the same God present in Christ — sovereign over all creaturely powers.
5: A shadow: Jonah is asleep below deck in the hold of the ship while the storm rages — the guilty prophet at rest while the innocent suffer. This is the negative form of Christ's sleep in the storm (Mark 4:38). Jonah's sleep is the sleep of one fleeing responsibility; Christ's sleep is the sleep of one whose identity as Lord of creation means he does not fear what the disciples fear. The captain's words to Jonah (“How can you sleep? Get up!”) are the obverse of the disciples' cry to Christ (“Teacher, do you not care that we are perishing?”); both scenes expose who the sleeping man really is.
6: A revelation of God: the captain's desperate plea — “Perhaps your god will give a thought to us, so that we do not perish” — is the voice of humanity in extremis, calling to any god who might hear. The Gentile captain does not know YHWH but gropes toward the possibility of divine mercy. Christ is the answer to this universal groping: “I am the door; if anyone enters by me, he will be saved” (John 10:9). The “perhaps” of the Gentile captain becomes the “whoever believes” of John 3:16 — the uncertainty of natural religious instinct replaced by the certainty of the word of Christ.
7: A theme: the casting of lots to identify the guilty one resonates with the NT's crucifixion scene, where lots are cast — but over Christ's clothing (Matthew 27:35, fulfilling Psalms 22:18), not to identify him. The contrast matters: lots identify Jonah as guilty; Christ's identity needed no lots because he was publicly condemned by accusation, mockery, and the judicial process. Jonah hides his guilt; Christ's innocence is publicly declared by Pilate (“I find no basis for a charge against this man”, Luke 23:4) even as he is condemned. What the process of identification reveals is different in each case.
8: A theme: Jonah's confession — “I am a Hebrew, and I fear the LORD, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land” — is theologically precise but situationally ironic; he fears the God he is fleeing. The irony illuminates a genuine spiritual condition: knowing the truth about God without living in conformity to it. Christ, by contrast, is the one in whom profession and practice are perfectly unified — he is the truth (John 14:6), not merely one who knows it. The gap between Jonah's confession and conduct is the gap that Christ's incarnation bridges: he is what Jonah claimed to be.
9: A theme: the sailors' “what have you done?” in response to Jonah's flight is the voice of moral perception — the Gentiles recognize the gravity of what has happened before Jonah acknowledges it. This inverts the expected hierarchy (Jew > Gentile in moral perception). Christ's parable of the Good Samaritan enacts the same inversion: the one who shows mercy is the despised outsider (Luke 10:33-37). The Gentiles who fear YHWH in this chapter prefigure the Gentiles who receive the gospel more readily than those to whom it first came (Matthew 21:43).
10: A revelation of God: the sailors' fear when they hear that Jonah has fled from YHWH's presence — “What is this you have done?” — expresses reverence for the inescapability of YHWH's authority. The NT's form of this recognition is the disciples' “Who then is this, that even wind and sea obey him?” (Mark 4:41) and Peter's “Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord” (Luke 5:8). Holiness that produces awe in those who encounter it is consistent across the testaments — and it reaches its fullest expression in the incarnate Son.
11: A theme: the sailors asking “what shall we do with you, that the sea may quiet down for us?” is the practical question of rescue: what action will bring peace to the chaos? This is, structurally, the question of salvation — what must be done to be saved? Jonah's answer (throw me into the sea) is the shadow of the gospel answer: the one who is given up for the many. But the contrast is maintained: Jonah is thrown because of his own guilt; Christ gave himself voluntarily for ours (Galatians 2:20: “who loved me and gave himself for me”).
12: A type: “Pick me up and hurl me into the sea; then the sea will quiet down for you, for I know it is because of me that this great tempest has come upon you.” This is the book's most direct structural type of substitutionary atonement: one man identifies himself as the cause of the storm that threatens the many; one man is given up; the storm ceases; the many are saved. Christ is the antitype with the crucial difference: Jonah is guilty, Christ is innocent (2 Corinthians 5:21: “God made him who knew no sin to be sin for us”). The self-offering of the guilty one becomes in Christ the self-offering of the sinless one — the only substitution that can truly satisfy.
13: A theme: the sailors “rowed hard to get back to dry land, but they could not” — their effort to avoid the cost of the solution, to find another way. The NT equivalent is the disciples' inability to heal the demonized boy (Matthew 17:16: “they could not heal him”) and Peter's doomed attempt to walk on water before faith falters (Matthew 14:30). Human effort to avoid or achieve the solution to the human problem consistently fails. Only the action of YHWH — whether commanding the sea to calm or raising Christ from the dead — accomplishes what human effort cannot.
14: A revelation of God: the sailors' prayer before throwing Jonah overboard — “O LORD, let us not perish for this man's life, and lay not on us innocent blood” — is one of the most precise moral prayers in Scripture. They ask YHWH to absorb the guilt of their necessary action. This is structurally the prayer that Pilate's handwashing attempts to express (Matthew 27:24: “I am innocent of this man's blood”) — but at the crucifixion it is YHWH himself who bears the “innocent blood” through the death of his Son, so that those who call on his name need not carry it (Acts 2:38).
15: A type: “So they picked up Jonah and hurled him into the sea, and the sea ceased from its raging.” Jonah enters the sea; the storm ceases; the sailors are saved. The typological structure is explicit in Jesus's identification (Matthew 12:40), but the pattern itself is the logic of substitutionary atonement embedded in narrative: the handing over of one person into judgment results in peace for the many. At the cross, Christ is handed over (Romans 4:25: “who was delivered over to death for our sins”) into the sea of divine judgment — and the result is reconciliation, peace with God (Romans 5:1).
16: A shadow: the sailors “feared the LORD greatly, and they offered a sacrifice to the LORD and made vows.” Gentile sailors come to faith in YHWH through the crisis they experienced with Jonah — the one whose disobedience threatened their lives paradoxically becomes the instrument of their conversion. This anticipates the Gentile mission: through the death and resurrection of the one who “went down” (like Jonah), countless Gentiles come to worship YHWH. Cornelius's household (Acts 10), the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8), and the Greek God-fearers (Acts 17:4) are the fullness of what these sailors begin.
